item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the ideas in this, but it didn't make sense until I read explanations of it, so I'm downvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe you can't find it because it isn't a novel at all. That sounds like a compilation of conspiracy theories, or short stories about conspiracy theories. The alien autopsy would be Roswell, and the presidential assassination would be JFK. Along with the moon landing, those are the most well-known sources of conspiracy theories.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well I just wanted to say that this is a great SCP, and that this is one of the best examples of pataphysical fiction I’ve read on the site. But, I also want to say that I feel as though I’ve experienced something like this before. I swear I’m not roleplaying.

I have this very distinct memory of this Novel, which I cannot remember the name of for the life of me, I can only remember certain aspects of it, and its mention by other people.

All that I know of the Novel is that it:
-Contains 24-26 chapters.
-Each chapter is its own self-contained, fully coherent story, you do not need to read any other chapters to understand just one.
-All Chapters are interconnected however, and It will still make sense if you read them in order.
-it will also make sense if you do not read them in order, such as reading chapter 1 then reading chapter 17, or vice versa, any combination of chapters can be read and it will still make sense.
-There is a Prime order, or a preferred order in which to read the chapters, I cannot remember it.
-I only know of 2 chapter descriptions, one chapter is about an alien autopsy, while another is about the president of the united states being a central character. (I think it is about their assassination from their POV?)
-The only way I know of this novel is a mention of it on the Vsauce YouTube group, I can clear as day recall Jake from Vsauce3 mentioning the novel in a video, describing the two chapters I mentioned.

This is all I know about this unnamed novel, whenever I try to find the video it is mentioned in, I cannot find it. I just think it is quite strange that I personally have experienced a remarkably similar phenomena depicted in this SCP, not saying I did, I acknowledge that my experience is different than the SCP, but still.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
god the only way this would get better is if the SCPs stop after series 6 …

and wait, there's the whole Russian copyright thing going on right now. holy crap.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It feels weird to say given how well-known it is, but this may the most underrated SCP on the site. Its score should be about 1000 votes higher than where it is. One of the few meta articles that I can say is an untouchable classic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Delightfully meta in every way! Bravo!

item.title: Necropost but...
item.content:encoded:
Well, you just had a really cool story idea/epiphany, and your comment has seven footnotes, made on the 7th of September, 2017 and last edited on the 27th…

f

item.title: I mean...
item.content:encoded:
Take a look at the affected entries footnote…

Edit: Didn't see the date; wasn't intending to necropost

item.title: spoiler ( i guess?)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

gabriel garcia marquez is now dead — this, plus how radiohead is disbanded and how satoshi kon is dead, was meant to suggest that the creators of the cursed works are the first to go, having first envisioned the relevant elements together in their heads (the raw space where stories thrive)

item.title: Another phricken someauthordude fan theory
item.content:encoded:
spoilers ahead

Drop down dropdown
Collapse collapsible

SCP-2747 and the Abrahamic creation myth are connected, but opposite.

There are seven days of creation, the seventh of which has no actual creation. This connects to 2747's association with seven.

God as depicted in the Bible is associated with light and fire (I mean seriously He loves using it, just look at the destruction of Sodom), while 2747 is associated with darkness.

My idea is that narratives can appear in a similar fashion (?) to how 2747 deletes them. This is consistent with the Old Testament/Torah appearing out of nowhere. It just appeared itself into existence.

Other creation hypotheses associated with light may be based on this.

Make of this what you will.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"when questioned under duress, they deny having written affected material and deny the existence of fictional works mentioned therein"

But what about questioning alleged authors? The first example, a wikipedia page, names an author of the nonexistent work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I finished it, was underwhelmed by the twist, and assumed I had missed something. On looking through the comments it turns out I didn't. The Foundation is fictional and in including 2747 destroys itself starting with the addendum, or alternatively the addendum is the only part to be destroyed since it's an account of the item (more likely the former, when in doubt go with the spookier one).
I dont like the fictional accounts. They don't feel like they're unified at all other than being Mandela syndrome cases with something black in them. I'm not sure how the Foundation drew the conclusion that they're all the same and not connected to any of the other retrocausal items going around- erasing from reality isn't exactly a one off effect.
For that matter, why was the addendum only deleted and not completely erased from history? The other forms of media didn't become blank disks and pages, they were removed from.existence entirely.
I'm not sure if I'm -1ing because I'm a reactionary jerk or just because I don't like it, but I'm -1ing either way. This doesn't do anything for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Addendum B isn't an experiment to create an instance of 2747, it is supposed to contain information regarding the nature of 2747 the Foundation has gained through creating various meta-narratives. However, even just this description of 2747 was enough to activate it, causing the whole addendum to be annihilated. And per the article:

It can effect through layers of metafictional narrative, i.e. a metanarrative containing the anafabula will cease to exist within the narrative, followed by the narrative itself disappearing

The next ones on the chopping block are going to be the Foundation, as they too are fictional.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't see what the point of this SCP is. I've read it many times, I don't get it. Everybody in the comments are saying that it's really great, and there's a huge twist, but I don't see one.

They said that they were trying to recreate the requirements to have a story deleted from reality, and the experiment was done in addendum B. They said they succeeded, and I can see that addendum B is gone. Whoop de doo. They made a story and now the story doesn't exist. This just seems underwhelming.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When matter metantimatter.
#drops mic
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's all been said before by other people, but I can't let it go on without restating it: this is excellent. It took a couple of read-throughs to fully appreciate how excellent it truly is, but by the end I was left dumbfounded at both the creativity of the concept and the finesse of the execution. It's one of those ideas that left me wanting to pick the author's brain to find whatever well of creativity they drank from to come up with this in the first place. I'm trying to reverse-engineer the evolution of this idea to whatever proto-narrative spawned it, and I just can't, it's too complete.

…Maybe I could find it if I saw the evolution of a few other narratives into a similar concept. Six of them would probably be enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I combed through the site after finally getting an account just to upvote this. Easily in the top 5 favorite SCPs for me.

item.title: SCP is satisfactory this one is my
item.content:encoded: This is definitely my favourite SCP thus far. The fact that its actual file mirrors the nature of the SCP (without massive banners saying the file has been corrupted, etc.) is genius. Its much bigger than it seems and it perfectly captures the feelings of intrigue and mild fear that most high quality SCPs carry. Amazing work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoyed your explanation. I thought it was a neat article, but didn't quite get it. Your breakdown makes me enjoy this SCP many times more than I did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Its definitely a reference, as stated by SunnyClockwork in the discussion’s author post.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Real nice skip! Just wondering if it had any connection to the “horned black” that the fifthtists has to deal with in one tale: Why is Five afraid of Seven? or if it’s just a neat coincidence

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This and 2614 are pretty similar…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sure. There a whole bunch of concepts here, but what really stands out is the idea of stories-within-stories. Another important bit is the suggestion that this wiki itself is fictional, and the possibility that "real reality" is in danger due to threats of the "fictional realities" created in the "real reality". There are already a lot of articles about these themes. Here's a heavily spoilery hint as to where the highest concentration of them is, before I outright list them.

+ Where to find similar entries
- End

Read the 001 proposals.

+ Specific examples
- End

There are FIVE entries that come to mind immediately, and they're all 001s. I've only read about half the 001s, as I'm trying to spread them out, so I would not be shocked if there are even more. The 001 slots are definitely among the most attention-grabbing articles on the site by virtue of their entry number alone, so while obvious effort was put into 2747, it just didn't seem anywhere near as novel as I'd heard. The main thing that seemed different was just the intensity and density of the obfuscation layered on, and it was a bummer for me to slowwwwly peel those layers away and find something I'd already found elsewhere quite a few times.

Primarily, this entry seems extraordinarily similar to wmdd's Project Palisade 001 proposal. Stories are being eaten by some cosmic horror that eats fictional universes, and the only known defense is to make decoy stories. I was annoyed at finding what seemed to be a rehash. HOWEVER! Much to my surprise, while researching for the post I am writing just now, 2747 seems to have been created before Project Palisade. Like, really similar. Huh. Is this SCP included in Project Palisade somewhere and I'm just not seeing it?

Here's the entry page of Project Palisade -
http://www.scp-wiki.net/wmdd-s-proposal

Here are two more 001s which have the same metafictional story-within-a-story, in-universe recognition of the collections of the SCP wiki being fiction -

http://www.scp-wiki.net/sandrewswann-s-proposal
http://www.scp-wiki.net/i-h-p-proposal

And two 001s which imply the Foundation itself is fictitious or anomalous in nature -

http://www.scp-wiki.net/jonathan-ball-s-proposal
http://www.scp-wiki.net/scantron-s-proposal

Meanwhile, we've got even more stories about characters who realize they're characters in stories on this wiki-

http://www.scp-wiki.net/metafiction
http://www.scp-wiki.net/theselfinsert
http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-3999 (I think)
http://www.scp-wiki.net/operation-overmeta

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You should check out The Raw Shark Texts - it's about a conceptual shark that preys on identity.

I wouldn't say this is common, because I've looked and looked for other books with this type of sinister abstract magical realism and never found them (RST is easily in my top 5 books of all time and had a huge influence on my own writing). The only other similar thing I can think of is House of Leaves, which is deals with some of the same themes of annihilation and uses similar metafictional devices.

All of these including 2747 are great and distinctive enough that I don't think you can compare any of them unfavorably to each other. I would love to hear about other ones myself because I'm always looking for more of this kind of material.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If the general idea is nothing new, I'm actually quite interested to hear about other similar fictions :o Could you point me towards some recommendations?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay; I appreciate that. And sorry if I was a bit harsh about it — I just really dislike that sentiment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes indeed, that was uncalled for and more caustic than at all necessary. Cranky about a trend I don't like and was taking it out on this article in particular, which isn't fair at all. Thanks for pointing that out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The meta is just way too dense here to be good reading

The meta is scarcely there at all; it's at best implied — nothing more. A completely valid interpretation of this skip involves not even acknowledging the meta component.

The unending, glowing praise for this article honestly seems to be people praising themselves for being able to understand the subtle nuances and ineffable blah blah blah.

And I could say that all you're doing is praising yourself for being able to "see beyond the hype". Such an accusation would be equally ridiculous and baseless.

This piece isn't hard or complex. If anything, it's just your basic setup and punchline (with lots of Easter eggs along the way). People don't enjoy it because they want to feel smart or look clever — they enjoy it because they enjoy it. That's all.

Criticizing articles you don't like is fine (if anything, it's critical! Feedback is the lifeblood of good writing), but don't insult people for gushing over something you don't enjoy. It's petty and rude — and just makes you out to be the one putting on "airs" of "intelligence".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Edit - Sorry, I was a jerk. I'm not liking the trend I (think I) am seeing with articles being so verbose, especially when I see them as meta. Obviously a lot of people feel differently.

The meta is just way too dense here to be good reading, hard for the sake of being hard, and the general idea is nothing new either. The unending, glowing praise for this article honestly seems to be people praising themselves for being able to understand the subtle nuances and ineffable blah blah blah. The only challenge is that it is presented in a way that makes it time consuming to reread the time or two necessary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I avoid commenting on widely discussed articles unless I have anything new to say… but I just revisited this for the first time in a long while and all I want to do is gush about how fucking brilliant it is. This is meta at its peak, IMO, and honestly don't see how it could ever be topped. Subtle, taut, confident; the meta aspect is not only justified but required, and the author trusts the reader to arrive at it on their own. Just outstanding.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Presuming SCP-2747 has already infected the Foundation, you could contain it by just endlessly producing a series of nested stories for it to devour. Just keep nesting narratives on top of narratives, with the beast constantly rising up through those layers trying to catch you.

How would you keep writing nested stories? Stockpiled writers. You don't need good ones, just enthusiastic ones. In other words, you need fanfiction writers. What would they write fanfiction about? 2747 itself — and the organization that contains it.

What would this look like? 2747 eats the narratives that contain it, so it would result in references to stories that don't exist, along with a confusing collaborative 'exterior' narrative filled with conflicting, contradictory elements that fit together thematically, but not canonically. Writers would spend increasing amounts of time working to patch these conflicts.

How would you know that 2747 was about to breach containment? The exterior narrative would become increasingly obsessed with its own fictitious nature.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The anafabula is already trapped in infinitely deep nested stories, and there is (presumably) no top for it to escape to. The problem is that the Foundation is one of these infinitely nested stories. To truly keep SCP-2747 contained, it has to be completely absent from the Foundation's narrative. Expunging it completely from all records might do the trick. Even then, some curious goof might ruin everything by trying to uncover the anomaly again, not knowing how dangerous it is. That's the problem with containing ideas.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want to listen to this album while drinking a latte-art recreation of the cover.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is hands-down one of my favorite articles on the site.

On a side note, I can't help but wonder if the house from House of Leaves was an actual anomaly that ended up getting erased by this thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you. I did not get it myself. I am too stupid for this unfortunately. :(

So, after it destroys the SCP universe, it would destroy our universe too, because ours is the next layer? Spooky.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This skip has everything that we1 dislike about the Pataphysics Department put together.

I would love for you to elaborate on this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think you should speak for the majority considering how well received this has been.

Besides, the meta aspect of this article is only one aspect of its brilliance. It effectively mimics multiple writing styles, has some very smart in universe ideas like the mechanics of Lucid Chalice, and has a fantastic mystery build up with a powerful climax.

Meta is only bad when it is lazy fourth wall breaking or shoehorned in. Neither is the case here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not this article's fault that people write poorly-received, bad, or overdone variations of meta.

Just because a thing was done a lot doesn't make that thing bad, or the source inspiration for those things bad. It just makes it the originator. This is a very clever, tightly-written, and well-executed article that doesn't rely on any gimmicks or tricks because… again, it was the originator, so it had no tricks to rely on; just very good writing. Also, saying that an article has "aged poorly" despite it only being around for (not even) two years is pretty wild. This article hasn't even gotten a chance to age; there's no way it can age poorly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Even though this has the elusive and fabled Kalinin upvote, I honestly can't like this one

Now I get that this was written in '16 when meta skips were, IMO, at their peak and not overused and disliked as they are today.

At the time, this and Swann's 001 were revolutionary because they explored the idea that the Foundation is fictitious and/or controlled by us, the writers. In 2018, this idea is heavily disliked and overused due to the Pataphysics Department coming along

This skip has everything that we1 dislike about the Pataphysics Department put together.

Again, I'll say I know this was made in 2016, but honestly this has aged poorly

-1

Footnotes
1. as in, the majority of this Wiki in 2018

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: its too late

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know how to contain it. A recursive narrative, a story with itself, that way it’s trapped in an infinitely nested story, no matter how much it keeps eating it will never reach the top and escape. Absolute containment achieved, problem solved. Either that or it would eat itself

item.title: Re: +300!!!!
item.content:encoded:
is that a haiku
you clever Ｍｏｔｈｅｒｆｕｃｋｅｒ
it's snowing on Mt. Fuji

whoever knew a
tlon uqbar orbis tertius
straight ripoff could work?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright, this is it. This is the greatest work of metafiction this site will ever produce, surpassing even S. Andrew Swann's proposal.

No matter how hard any of us try, I firmly believe no 4th wall breaking will ever approach the subtlety and sophistication of this. Pack it up, 2747 is the death of meta.

(+1 +1 +1 +1 until I break the damn rating button)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After leaving for several years, this made me think 'wow what happened to this site?' in the best way possible and motivated me to start posting again.

Ok, in that regard maybe it has a lot to answer for, but enthusiastic +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ooh, very good! I’d been in the mire of early-3000s mediocrity until a chance link brought me here, and I was reminded why I love this site. The article creates a wonderful sense of foreboding coupled with strong believability from the academic language and yet despite that I still got a shock and—yes—a shiver down the spine when I revealed the final addendum.

I was all ready to upvote anyway when I got to

now-defunct English rock band Radiohead

and mentally cried “No!”.

My only complaint is that the Scolipendra is a bit too unsubtle, but it does work within context, fitting the recursion-with-corruptions theme very nicely.

item.title: Re: What?
item.content:encoded:
It's not a new twist if you have been to the site for a while, but it's quite well executed, regardless. Not seeing people being pretentious about it either.

The appeal of the article to me, as somebody who read the description and immediately got the nagging feeling there wouldn't be an Appendix B, is that it deals with something very weird and very hard to contain from a convincing in-universe POV.

This might not be one of the most threatening or scary Keter objects on the site but, if you take a step back a think a little about the implications it would have to normalcy at large, it's a total mindfuck. Also, considering it's effecting its own documentation, containment isn't going too well.

All around, a very well written meta article that doesn't rely on format screw.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For anyone who's interested, the instance Taitoru by Satoshi Kon in this article is extremely similar to Kon's unfinished manga, Opus, a highly recommended read that shares a bit of parallels with this article (including the unfinished final chapter Dark Horses included in the English publication). I/O, though pretty different, is somewhat similar in tone to A Moon Shaped Pool also by Radiohead. Even though minmin claims to have done this without having read or heard of these, I fully believe both capture the mood and essence of this article.

item.title: Re: What?
item.content:encoded:
It’s basically an anti-narrative. Since the SCP article is a narrative, the effect is taking place within the article.

why so smug?

I haven’t seen any smugness around here, and I don’t even like the article.

item.title: What?
item.content:encoded:
Did I miss something? Am I just dumb? I read through the thing, then clicked addendum B, and I got "Data Lost". Clicking it again returns it to it's former state. "Haha" I thought "very clever, they talk about media that doesn't exist wherein the key piece of the article doesn't exist", then I enter the comments and everyone is going crazy about some subplot and how you need 200 IQ to understand this? What subplot? Why so smug?

Beyond that, the article is average. No vote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Once upon a time I took the footnote's link to SCP-2614 and noted the similarity of passing through different realities as nested narratives. The first few times I read it, I was confused about the end of the nested mimetic log.1 I had no idea what it all meant, but it obviously has terrifying implications for the Foundationverse. More recently, I have found this comment and its enlightening reply, to my delight.2

So, assuming that SCP-2747 is directly related to SCP-2614 in regards to pataphysics and nested narrative realities, which I have no doubt is the case, I infer the following:

+ Possible Ultimate Spoiler?
- Spekulah!

SCP-2747 is some unnamed demiurge entity. It probably is a "being" with consciousness or at least purpose, and it manifests in narratives, through narremes describing itself, as the antithesis to some completion/journey/discovery/quest/whatever involving an enlightenment, which is the main objective of the narrative in question.3

Any narrative referencing "the light" or is actually referring to this divine knowledge (which can be triggered in some way by what remains of a previous SCP-2747 instance)4, an awakening of sorts, which would also allow one to realize that the world is a narrative and what SCP-2747 actually is. SCP-2747 is the diabolus ex machina that stops that from happening, to uphold the illusion of reality and more specifically, to keep anyone from realizing that it's all just another work of fiction, nested among infinite others. And it will not even stop at that reality, as anybody in a related fiction one level upwards could pick up the pieces and also realize what's going on.5

The mountain is described in detail throughout the story, with recurring metaphors alternatingly alluding to it as either an abode of the gods or a demonic presence.

The game's non-playable characters continuously mention a "coalblack thornbound tome" that, when read from, would enable one to either obtain great power, or unleash a dreadful curse;

Mavigne, Or: A Treatise On The Metaphysics Of Inner Space Travel, And The Kingdom Of Erikaar, Whose Name Is Darkness Made Light, And Further Theological Expositions Thereof

The exact nature of Scolipendra's plot is hard to discern, as the various pieces of fanfiction sport vastly differing storylines and a range of character interpretations, further complicated by the involvement of other fictional universes and settings.

one scene features the protagonist physically grappling with the shadows in her apartment - described under the trope entry for "Your Mind Makes It Real" as the manifestation of her creative block - which eventually engulf and consume her.

In other words, SCP-2747 acts as some sort of gatekeeper guarding great epiphanies that cause one to transcend the mortal plane of narratives, and it does this by eating entire narratives. Or your memories. Or local reality. Or the universe. It depends on your perspective, given just how meta it is.6

One item features heavily throughout the compiled works: a 7-sided obsidian emblem said to possess the power to destroy any object, person, or abstract concept with a single touch. It appears to be of great interest to the protagonists, who repeatedly make attempts to acquire it; however, it also appears to be currently in the possession of a sinister unnamed antagonist who is alluded to repeatedly in several works, yet is never seen.

Presumably, there are 7 steps to enlightenment, which gives you the power to escape narratives.7 Unfortunately, as soon as one gazes towards the fourth wall, it gazes back. Of course, anyone who somehow manages to avoid narrative destruction could conceivably ascend to a higher plane of existence.


So, that's my idea. Can someone please tell me whether I hit the mark? minmin? Anyone?
Footnotes
1. "DATA CONCURRENT WITH PATAPHYSICAL HYPERMODEL: DMRG"
2. Thank you, phyrestorm and Scorpion451!
3. In simpler terms, it manifests as its own metaphor. Therefore, it is self-referential.
4. Other things can inspire such knowledge, but SCP-2747 commonly draws attention to its own true nature, which is why the anomaly inevitably ends up destroying narratives containing other narratives containing itself.
5. Like House of Leaves, except infinitely larger and crazier.
6. If that's too hard to visualize, just imagine that the matrix has its own debugging program, or something.
7. Actually, I'm still not sure why the number 7 is even relevent at all. Do you mind filling me in?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, okay. Which skips have interested you, then? I'd like to check those out as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I got that. I just didn't see what's so great about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The devil's in the details. Read through Appendix A, compare with the description, and consider the implications this would have.

Spoiler
– hide block

Think about it. The anomaly is made of narremes. Narremes are units of narrative. In other words, narremes are ideas, concepts.

The Foundation made a huge mistake. They had SCP-2747 properly contained, or at least as well as they could contain an idea. They then explored and fully described the nature of the anomaly, which was suicidal because ideas exist where they are invoked. The documentation is an instance of SCP-2747, because all the narremes are there. That's why Appendix B is empty. Subject to the anomaly, the document is now left hopelessly and mysteriously incomplete, missing just one piece of the puzzle!

By the way, all SCP-2747 instances are left incomplete, with no traditional ending and no certainty except oblivion, in universe and out. Also, all the narremes that make these narratives anomalous describe the anomaly itself in some way. Each narrative also contains 7 of something, perhaps symbolically, which in this case are the 7 listed SCP-2747 instances. Of course, the described instances have their own 7s, such as 7 main characters (6 protagonists and one murder victim, where the murderer is an elusive and malevolent personification of the anomaly).

The anomaly travels out of nested universes via metafiction. It's reached it's own documentation. Now, what's the next level of narrative?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: tfw someone spots the hidden numbers

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It contains 6 tracks measuring a total of 34 minutes and 18 seconds.

So each track is 7*7*7=343 seconds long? Nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe it's due to the hype I've heard about this or maybe I'm just incredibly thick in the head, but the skip didn't do anything for me. I got the ending, and my initial reaction was "ok. That's it?". I felt like there was supposed to be this giant reveal at the end, but there really wasn't. I'll admit, it's not badly written at all. I just don't think it's particularly interesting to me. Maybe it's due to me not understanding the technical jargon, or maybe it was due to me losing interest halfway through Appendix A.

Either way, I don't get what's so amazing about the article.

But, then again, my collective author stats is less than the entire article, so what the hell do I know.

item.title: Wow.
item.content:encoded: Brilliantly executed. I read the whole thing like 3 times before it completely hit me. I only even knew I was missing something once I reached the end. Excellent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Beyond these 6 realms lies another threshold, this time one of cold and silence, which is described to be the Earth's core; before it can be elaborated upon, Maas wakes, and the dream ends.

Dreams can end abruptly, and are rapidly forgotten after ending. This fits perfectly with the theme, and I found it odd that nobody else seemed to notice this particular piece of forshadowing. (Although it is far more subtle than, say, the existence of a "Scolipendra Wiki". And to be fair, that addendum is basically a very large wall of foreshadowing.)

That aside, this SCP is now my favorite, simply because of its sheer brilliance. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: See I knew that I couldn't have been the only one who saw that as well.
